Question: The correct order of procedures in DNA profiling is
Options: DNA isolation -> electrophoresis -> blotting -> restriction digestion -> autoradiography
DNA isolation-> restriction digestion -> electrophoresis -> blotting -> autoradiography
DNA isolation -> restriction digestion -> blotting -> electrophoresis -> autoradiography
Restriction digestion -> DNA isolation -> blotting-> electrophoresis
Solution:
The technique of DNA Fingerprinting was initially developed by Alec Jeffreys. It included: -
  •  Isolation of DNA
  •  Digestion of DNA by restriction endonucleases
  •  Separation of DNA fragments by electrophoresis
  • Transferring (blotting) of separated DNA fragments to synthetic membranes, such as nitrocellulose or nylon
  • Hybridisation using labelled VNTR probe
  • Detection of hybridised DNA fragments by autoradiography

Question: Which among the following statements depicts the function of insertional inactivation within the coding sequence of β-galactosidase enzyme?
Options: The recombinants and non-recombinants cannot be differentiated
The recombinants and non-recombinants can be differentiated on the basis of colour production
The recombinants can be differentiated on the basis of antibiotic resistance
The recombinants can be differentiated by the use of physical methods
Solution: Insertional inactivation occurs when recombinant DNA is inserted into the coding sequences of an enzyme, β-galactosidase which results in inactivation of the gene for the synthesis of the enzyme. This is known as insertional inactivation. The function of insertional inactivation is to differentiate the recombinants and non-recombinants based on color. Blue colour colonies are produced by non-recombinants after the addition of chromogenic substrate as the coding region is not interrupted with insert and hence active enzyme is formed. The enzyme acts on substrate and gives blue colour colonies. Recombinants produce white colour colonies as coding region of an enzyme is interrupted and hence no functional enzyme is not formed.

Question:
Assertion: ${K_p} = {K_c}$ for all reaction.
Reason: At constant temperature, the pressure of the gas is proportional to its concentration.
Options: If both assertion and reason are true and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If assertion is false but reason is true.
Solution:
Assertion is false but reason is true.
${K_p} \ne {K_c}$ for all reaction.

${K_p} = {K_c}{(RT)^{\Delta n}}$

$\Delta n = $ number of moles of products – number of moles of reactants in the balanced chemical equation.

So, if for a reaction $\Delta n = 0$. Then ${K_p} = {K_c}$


At constant temperature, the pressure of the gas is proportional to its concentration.

Question:
Two statements are given regarding oscillations:
I. If the mass of the bob of a simple pendulum is doubled, its time period remains unchanged.
II. If a spring-block system is taken into a satellite, its time period becomes infinite.
Options: I is correct, II is incorrect
I is incorrect, II is correct.
Solution: ${T_{{\text{pendulum}}}} = 2\pi \sqrt {L/g} $, which is independent of mass. ${T_{{\text{spring}}}} = 2\pi \sqrt {m/k} $, which is independent of gravity. Therefore, the spring-block system will oscillate normally in a satellite (${g_{{\text{eff}}}} = 0$ ), but the pendulum would stop (T becomes infinite).

Question: Endemic species are always at a higher risk of extinction than others because

They are exclusively found in only one place in the world
They are weaker in comparison to other non-endemic species

Question: How many moles of ${K_2}C{r_2}{O_7}$ in acidic medium are required to react completely with one mole of ${H_2}{C_2}{O_4}$

n-factor for K2Cr2O7 = 6

n-factor for H2C2O4 = 2

Number of equivalents must be equal for both compounds. Number of equivalents = Number of moles $\times$ n-factor

Thus, $6 \times x = 2 \times 1$

$x = \frac{1}{3}$

Question:
Match the AC circuit parameters/devices in Column I with their characteristics in Column II.
Column IColumn II
AResonance in Series RLCP${V_s}{I_s} = {V_p}{I_p}$ (Ideal)
BQuality Factor $\left( Q \right)$Q ${\text{cos}}\phi = 1$
CTransformerR $P = {V_{rms}}{I_{rms}}{\text{cos}}\phi $
DAverage Power in ACS $\frac{{{\omega _0}L}}{R}$

Which of the following is the correct match?
Solution:
At resonance, ${X_L} = {X_C}$, so the circuit is purely resistive and the power factor ${\text{cos}}\phi = 1$.
Quality Factor $Q$ is defined as the ratio of resonance frequency to bandwidth: $Q = \frac{{{\omega _0}L}}{R}$.
For an ideal transformer, input power equals output power $\left( {{V_p}{I_p} = {V_s}{I_s}} \right)$.
Average power in an AC circuit is ${P_{{\text{avg}}}} = {V_{rms}}{I_{rms}}{\text{cos}}\phi $.

Question: In a hydrogen atom, when an electron moves from the orbit n = 1 to n = 4, then wavelength of the radiations absorbed will be (R is Rydberg’s constant):
Solution:
Wavelength is given by

$$\frac{1}{\lambda } = R\left( {\frac{1}{{{n_1}^2}} - \frac{1}{{{n_2}^2}}} \right) \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\lambda } = R\left[ {\frac{1}{{{{\left( 1 \right)}^2}}} - \frac{1}{{{{\left( 4 \right)}^2}}}} \right]$$

$$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\lambda } = R\left( {\frac{1}{1} - \frac{1}{{16}}} \right) \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\lambda } = R\left( {\frac{{16 - 1}}{{16}}} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\lambda } = \frac{{15R}}{{16}} \Rightarrow \lambda  = \frac{{16}}{{15R}}$$

Read the following statements regarding bryophytes and select the correct answer.
(i) Lack true roots, stem and leaves.
(ii) Main plant body is haploid.
(iii) Sex-organs are unicellular and non-jacketed.
(iv) Fertilization produces an embryo inside the water.
Solution:
The plant body of bryophytes is thallus-like and prostrate or erect, attached to the substratum by unicellular or multicellular rhizoids. They lack true roots, stem or leaves. However, may possess root-like, leaf-like or stem-like structures. The main plant body of the bryophyte is haploid. 

<!-StartFragment->The sex organs in bryophytes are multicellular.
The male sex organ is called antheridium. They produce biflagellate
antherozoids. The female sex organ called archegonium is flask-shaped
and produces a single egg. The antherozoids are released into the water where
they come in contact with archegonium. An antherozoid fuses with the
egg to produce the zygote. Zygotes do not undergo reduction division
immediately. They produce a multicellular body called a sporophyte.<!-EndFragment-><!-EndFragment->

Question: A large horizontal surface moves up and down in SHM with an amplitude of 1 cm. If a mass of 10 kg (which is placed on the surface) is to remain continuously in contact with it, then maximum frequency of S.H.M will be approximately equal to (take g= 10m/s<sup>2</sup><font size="1">)</font>
Solution:
For body to remain in contact ${a_{\max }} = g$
$\therefore {\omega ^2}A = g \Rightarrow 4{\pi ^2}{n^2}A = g$
$ \Rightarrow {n^2} = \frac{g}{{4{\pi ^2}A}} = \frac{{10}}{{4{{(3.14)}^2}0.01}} = 25.36$ $ \Rightarrow n = 5\;Hz$ Approx.

Question:
What is the binding energy per nucleon of $_6{C^{12}}$nucleus?
Given, mass of ${C^{12}}\left( {{m_c}} \right) = 12.000\,u$
Mass of proton $\left( {{m_p}} \right) = 1.0078\,u$
Mass of neutron $\left( {{m_n}} \right) = 1.0087\,u$
and $1\,{\text{amu}}\, = 931.4\frac{{{\text{MeV}}}}{{{c^2}}}$
Solution:
Binding energy per nucleon
\[ = \frac{{\left[ {\left\{ {{m_p}Z + {m_n}\left( {A - Z} \right)} \right\} - M} \right] \times 931.4}}{{{\text{Mass number A}}}}\]
$ = \left( {\frac{{6 \times 1.0078 + 6 \times 1.0087 - 12}}{{12}}} \right) \times 931.4$
$ = 7.68\,{\text{MeV}}$

Question:
Select the correct/incorrect functions of the following enzymes
1. Ligase – Joins short DNA segments
2. DNA Polymerase – Cuts DNA at specific sites
3. Helicase - Breaks H-bonds between complementary DNA strands
Solution:
Ligase is a specific type of enzyme that facilitates the joining of DNA strands together by catalyzing the formation of a phosphodiester bond.
DNA polymerase is an enzyme that synthesizes DNA molecules from deoxyribonucleotides, the building blocks of DNA. These enzymes are essential for DNA replication and usually work in pairs to create two identical DNA strands from a single parent DNA molecule.
DNA helicase is an enzyme that functions by melting the hydrogen bonds that hold the DNA into the double helix structure, and hence help in unwinding the DNA.

Question: Two moles of $PC{l_5}$ is heated in a closed vessel of 2L capacity. When the equilibrium is attained 40% of it has been found to be dissociated. What is the  ${K_c}$ in mol/$d{m^3}?$
Solution:
Number of moles of $PC{l_5}$dissociated at equilibrium $ = 2 \times 40/100 = 0.8$
\[\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\mathop {PC{l_5}}\limits_{2\,mol} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, \rightleftharpoons \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\mathop {PC{l_3}}\limits_0 \,\,\,\,\,\,\, + \,\,\,\,\,\mathop {C{l_2}}\limits_0 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(initially)} \\
  {\,\,\left( {2 - 0.8} \right)\,mol\,\,\,\,\,\,\,0.8\,mol\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,0.8\,mol\,\,\,\,\,(at\,equilibrium)}
\end{array}\]
$\left[ {PC{l_5}} \right] = \frac{{1.2}}{2} = 0.6\,M{L^{ - 1}}$
$\left[ {PC{l_3}} \right] = \left[ {C{l_2}} \right] = \frac{{0.8}}{2} = 0.4\,M{L^{ - 1}}$
${K_c} = \frac{{\left[ {PC{l_3}} \right]\left[ {C{l_2}} \right]}}{{\left[ {PC{l_5}} \right]}} = \frac{{0.4 \times 0.4}}{{0.6}}$$ = 0.267\,\,mol/d{m^3}$

Question:
The equilibrium constant for the reaction: ${N_{2\left( g \right)}} + {O_{2\left( g \right)}} \rightleftharpoons 2N{O_{\left( g \right)}}$ at temperature T is $4 \times {10^{ - 4}}.$
The value of ${K_c}$ for the reaction, $N{O_{\left( g \right)}} \rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2}{N_{2\left( g \right)}} + \frac{1}{2}{O_{2\left( g \right)}}$
Solution:
\[{N_2} + {O_2} \rightleftharpoons 2NO\]
\[{K_{{C_1}}} = \frac{{{{\left[ {NO} \right]}^2}}}{{\left[ {{N_2}} \right]\left[ {{O_2}} \right]}} = 4 \times {10^{ - 4}}\]
\[NO \rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2}{N_2} + \frac{1}{2}{O_2}\]
\[{K_{{C_2}}} = \frac{{{{\left[ {{N_2}} \right]}^{\frac{1}{2}}}{{\left[ {{O_2}} \right]}^{\frac{1}{2}}}}}{{\left[ {NO} \right]}}\]
\[{K_{{C_2}}} = \sqrt {\frac{1}{{{K_{{C_2}}}}}}  = \sqrt {\frac{1}{{4 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}}  = 50\]

Question: The capacitors A and B are connected in series with a battery as shown in the figure. When the switch S is closed and the two capacitors get charged fully, then -

The potential difference across the plates of A is 6V and across the plates of B is 4V

In series $${q_A} = {q_B} = q = 10 \times \frac{{2 \times 3}}{5} \times {10^{ - 6}} = 12\mu C$$

$${V_A} = \frac{q}{{{C_A}}} = 6\,V$$                  $${V_B} = \frac{q}{{{C_B}}} = 4\,V$$

In series $$U \propto \frac{1}{C} \Rightarrow \frac{{{U_A}}}{{{U_B}}} = \frac{{{C_B}}}{{{C_A}}} = \frac{3}{2}$$

Question: The power factor of a series RL circuit is \[\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\]. If the frequency of the applied ac is doubled, the power factor becomes
Solution:
Power factor $$\cos \phi  = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{R}{Z};$$

$$\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{R}{{\sqrt {{R^2} + X_L^2} }} = \frac{R}{{\sqrt {{R^2} + {\omega ^2}{L^2}} }} \Rightarrow {\omega ^2}{L^2} = {R^2}$$

When the frequency $$\omega $$ is doubled,

$$\cos \phi  = \frac{R}{{\sqrt {{R^2} + 4{\omega ^2}{L^2}} }} = \frac{R}{{\sqrt {{R^2} + 4{R^2}} }}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \cos \phi  = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 5 }}$$

Hotspots are regions with high biodiversity and endemic species.

Question:
For the reaction \[{N_2} + 3{H_2} \rightleftharpoons 2N{H_3},\] the equilibrium constant is K1 and it is K2 for the reaction \[{N_2} + 2{H_2} \rightleftharpoons N{H_2} - N{H_2}\]. The equilibrium constant for the reaction
\[N{H_3} + {H_2}N - N{H_2} \rightleftharpoons \frac{3}{2}{N_2} + \frac{7}{2}{H_2}\] will be
Solution:
\[{N_2} + 3{H_2} \rightleftharpoons 2N{H_3},\left( {{K_1}} \right)\]

\[2N{H_3} \rightleftharpoons {N_2} + 3{H_2}\left( {\frac{1}{{{K_1}}}} \right)\]

\[N{H_3} \rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2}{N_2} + \frac{3}{2}{H_2}\left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt {{K_1}} }}} \right)....\left( 1 \right)\]

\[{N_2} + 2{H_2} \rightleftharpoons N{H_2} - N{H_2}\left( {{K_2}} \right)\]

\[N{H_2} - N{H_2} \rightleftharpoons {N_2} + 2{H_2}\left( {\frac{1}{{{K_2}}}} \right)....\left( 2 \right)\]

\[{\text{Add}}\left( 1 \right)\& \left( 2 \right)\]

\[N{H_3} + {H_2}N - N{H_2} \rightleftharpoons \frac{3}{2}{N_2} + \frac{7}{2}{H_2}\left( {\frac{1}{{{K_2}\sqrt {{K_1}} }}} \right)\]

Question:

Options: If assertion and reason is true and reason is correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion and reason is true and reason is not correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true and reason is false.
If both assertion and reason is false.
Solution: Mendel's experiments showed that the F1 progeny typically exhibit the dominant trait of the parents while the recessive trait is not visible. The assertion correctly states that the F1 progeny resemble one of the parents, which is a reflection of Mendelian inheritance. The reason explains this by noting that F1 progeny have both traits, but the dominant trait masks the recessive one, providing a correct explanation for the assertion.

Question: Which of the following statements is true with reference to cross pollination?
Options: It most often results in lower yield of plants
It occurs only in unisexual flowers
It always overcomes the distance barrier
It requires production of large number of pollen grains
Solution: Cross pollination involves the transfer of pollen grains from one plant to a compatible stigma of the flower of another plant. Since there are more chances of loss of pollen grains without reaching the compatible flower, cross pollination requires the production of a large number of pollen grains.

Question: All the following statements represent essential stages that takes place during meiosis, except:
Options: Two successive divisions without any DNA replication occurring between them.
Formation of chiasmata and crossing over.
Number of chromosomes in daughter cells after meiosis II remains the same as the parent cell.
Solution:
During meiosis, two successive divisions take place without any DNA replication occurring in-between. The number of chromosomes is reduced to half during meiosis I and not meiosis II.  In meiosis I, the number of chromosomes in a cell is reduced to half and the chromatids move to opposite spindle poles.  It is in meiosis II that the chromosomes are distributed to the gametes, one to each of the four gametes formed.  
After the G1 phase, the DNA content gets doubled in the S-phase.  After meiosis I, the DNA content get halved and after meiosis II, the DNA content further decreases by a factor of 2 to produce four haploid cells.  Therefore, meiosis II is equational division, having the same number of chromosomes but reduced DNA content.

Correct answer: (C)

Question: The potential energy of an electron in the Bohr orbit in the He<sup>+</sup> ion is
Solution:
Potential energy = -2× total energy
\[ = - 2 \times \left( {\frac{{13.6\,{Z^2}}}{{{n^2}}}} \right)\]
For helium, Z = 2 and n = 1 in first Bohr orbit
Thus, potential energy
 \[\begin{gathered}
   = - 2 \times \frac{{13.6 \times {{\left( 2 \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( 1 \right)}^2}}} \\
   = - 108.8\,eV \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question:
Read the given statements.
(i) Flat membranous sacs in the stroma of chloroplasts
(ii) Infoldings in mitochondria
(iii) Disc-shaped sacs in Golgi apparatus
Select the correct option as per the codes given above.
Solution:
Chloroplasts are double membrane-bound. The space limited by the inner membrane of the chloroplast is called the stroma. A number of organised flattened membranous sacs called the thylakoids are present in the stroma. The inner membrane of mitochondria forms a number of infoldings called the cristae (sing.: crista) towards the matrix. The cristae increase the surface area. Golgi apparatus consists of many flat, disc-shaped sacs or cisternae of 0.5μm to 1.0μm
diameter. These are stacked parallel to each other. A varied number of cisternae are present in a Golgi apparatus.

\[{ {\Delta H}} - \Delta {\text{U}} = \Delta {{\text{n}}_g}{\text{RT}}\]
\[{\text{C + }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}} \to {\text{CO}}\]

\[ = - 1238.78{\text{ Jmo}}{{\text{l}}^{{\text{ - 1}}}}\]

Question:

Statement 2: Haemoglobin molecule is composed of four polypeptide chains – two α-chains and two β-chains.
Options: Statement 1 is correct and statement 2 is incorrect
<!-StartFragment->Statement 1 is incorrect and statement 2 is correct<!-EndFragment->
Solution: Proteins are said to have quaternary structure if they consist of 2 or more polypeptide chains united by forces other than covalent bonds (i.e., not peptide or disulphide bonds). The forces that stabilize these aggregates are hydrogen bonds and electrostatic bonds formed between amino acids on the polypeptide chains, Haemoglobin, the oxygen-transporting protein of blood, is an example of quaternary structure. This protein consists of four peptide chains of two types-α and β chains that occur in pairs.

Question: Hyperconjugation usually involves the delocalisation of
Solution: Hyperconjugation involves the delocalisation of σ bond orbital. It is the stabilising interaction that results from the interaction of the electrons in a σ-bond (usually C-H or C-C) with an adjacent empty or partially filled p-orbital or a π*-orbital to give an extended molecular orbital that increases the stability of the system.

Question: The number of possible isomers of a square planar complex [M(ABCD)] is
Solution:
The given complex is a square planar complex of the type [M(ABCD)], where A, B, C, and D are four different monodentate ligands.

To find the number of possible geometrical isomers, we can fix the position of one ligand and then arrange the other three ligands in the remaining positions.

Let's fix the position of ligand A at the top position. Now, we consider which ligand is placed in the position trans (opposite) to A.

  1. Case 1: Ligand B is trans to A.
    The remaining ligands, C and D, will be trans to each other.
    The trans pairs are (A, B) and (C, D).

    A
    |
    C—M—D
    |
    B
  2. Case 2: Ligand C is trans to A.
    The remaining ligands, B and D, will be trans to each other.
    The trans pairs are (A, C) and (B, D).

    A
    |
    B—M—D
    |
    C
  3. Case 3: Ligand D is trans to A.
    The remaining ligands, B and C, will be trans to each other.
    The trans pairs are (A, D) and (B, C).

    A
    |
    B—M—C
    |
    D

These three arrangements are structurally different and thus represent three distinct geometrical isomers. No other arrangements are possible. For example, if we start by fixing B and place A trans to it, we get the same isomer as in Case 1.

Note: A square planar complex of type [M(ABCD)] is chiral and each of these 3 geometrical isomers should have an enantiomer. However, the question asks for the number of possible isomers and the options (1, 2, 3, 4) strongly suggest it is asking for the number of geometrical isomers, which is a standard result for this type of complex.

Therefore, there are 3 possible geometrical isomers.

The correct option is (b) 3.

Question: When two similar species live in the same area, they may evolve to become more different in order to
Options: Drive the other species to extinction
Use up the other species resources
Solution: According to the mechanism of resource partitioning (supported by Mac Arthur), if two species compete for the same resources, they could avoid competition by choosing, for instance, different times for feeding or different foraging patterns. Two species evolve to become different to reduce competition, so that both species can coexist.

Question: Which is incorrect about a small pond ecosystem?
Options: This is fairly a self-sustainable unit
All the four basic components of an ecosystem are well exhibited
The abiotic component is the water with all the dissolved organic and inorganic substances and the rich soil deposit at the bottom of the pond
This ecosystem performs all the functions of any ecosystem except for the unidirectional flow of energy
Solution: A small pond ecosystem is fairly a self-sustainable unit. The abiotic component is the water with all the dissolved organic and inorganic substances and the rich soil deposit at the bottom of the pond. All the four basic components of an ecosystem are well exhibited but it can't perform all the functions of any ecosystem.

(v) The patient of this disease is not required to be treated with antibiotics

Question: A spherical ball of radius 3.0 × 10–4 m and density 104 kg m–3 falls freely under gravity through a distance h before entering a tank of water. If after entering the water, the velocity of the ball does not change, what is the value of h (Viscosity of water is 9.8 × 10-6 N s m-2)? \[\left( {g = 9.8\,m/{s^2},{\rho _{{H_2}O}} = 1000\,kg/{m^3}} \right)\]
Solution:
Before entering the water the velocity of ball is \[\sqrt {2gh} .\] If

after entering the water this velocity does not change, then

this value should be equal to terminal velocity. Therefore,

\[\sqrt {2gh}  = \frac{2}{9}\frac{{{r^2}\left( {\rho  - \sigma } \right)g}}{\eta }\]

\[\therefore \,h = \frac{{{{\left\{ {\frac{2}{9}\frac{{{r^2}\left( {\rho  - \sigma } \right)g}}{\eta }} \right\}}^2}}}{{2g}} = \frac{2}{{81}} \times \frac{{{r^4}{{\left( {\rho  - \sigma } \right)}^2}g}}{{{\eta ^2}}}\]

\[ = \frac{2}{{81}} \times \frac{{{{\left( {3 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}} \right)}^4}{{\left( {{{10}^4} - {{10}^3}} \right)}^2} \times 9.8}}{{{{\left( {9.8 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} \right)}^2}}} = 1.65 \times {10^3}m\]

Question: The sporozoites that cause infection when a female Anopheles mosquito bites a human being are stored in:
Solution: Plasmodium enters the human body as sporozoites (infectious form) through the bite of infected female Anopheles mosquito. The parasites initially multiply within the liver cells and then attack the red blood cells (RBCs) resulting in their rupture. The rupture of RBCs is associated with the release of a toxic substance, hemozoin, which is responsible for the chill and high fever recurring every three to four days. When a female Anopheles mosquito bites an infected person, these parasites enter the mosquito's body and undergo further development. The parasites multiply within them to form sporozoites that are stored in their salivary glands. When these mosquitoes bite a human, the sporozoites are introduced into his/ her body, thereby initiating the events mentioned above.

Question:
Consider the following statements:
(1) $IO_3^ - $and $Xe{O_3}$are isostructural.
(2) $PF_6^ - $and $S{F_6}$are isostructural.
(3) $BH_4^ - $and $NH_4^ + $are isostructural.
(4) $CO_3^{2 - }$and $NO_2^ - $are isostructural.
Solution:
$IO_3^ - $, $Xe{O_3}$→ $s{p^3}$, pyramidal → isostructural
$PF_6^ - $, $S{F_6}$→ $s{p^3}{d^2}$, octahedral → isostructural
$BH_4^ - $, $NH_4^ + $→ $s{p^3}$, tetrahedral → isostructural
$CO_3^{2 - }$→ $s{p^2}$, trigonal planar
$NO_2^ - $→ $s{p^2}$, bent (due to lone pair) → not isostructural
Hence, statements (1), (2) and (3) are correct.

Question:
Statement I: The coefficient of static friction (${\mu _s}$) between two surfaces is always less than the coefficient of kinetic friction $\left( {{\mu _k}} \right)$.
Statement II: Friction force acting on a block kept on a horizontal surface always acts in the direction of the applied force to oppose the relative motion.
In light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
Options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true.
Both Statement I and Statement II are false.
Statement I is true but Statement II is false.
Statement I is false but Statement II is true.
Solution: Statement I is false because static friction is generally greater than kinetic friction (${\mu _s} > {\mu _k}$) as it takes more force to start motion than to maintain it. Statement II is false because friction always acts opposite to the direction of relative motion (or intended motion), not in the direction of the applied force.

Question: For a simple pendulum, a graph is plotted between its Kinetic Energy (KE) and Potential Energy (PE) against its displacement d. Which one of the following represents these correctly? (graphs are schematic and not drawn to scale)
Solution:
Taking minimum potential energy at mean position to be zero,

the expression of KE and PE are

$KE = \frac{1}{2}m{\omega ^2}\left( {{A^2} - {d^2}} \right){\text{and}}\,PE = \frac{1}{2}m{\omega ^2}{d^2}$

Both graphs are parabolas. 

At d = 0 (the mean position),

$PE = 0\,\,{\text{and}}\,KE = \frac{1}{2}m{\omega ^2}{A^2} = {\text{maximum}}$

At $d =  \pm A$ (the extreme positions),

$KE = 0\,\,{\text{and}}\,PE = \frac{1}{2}m{\omega ^2}{A^2} = {\text{maximum}}$

Therefore, the correct graph is (a).

Question: A force $\vec F = \alpha \hat i + 3\hat j + 6\hat k$ is acting at a point $\vec r = 2\hat i - 6\hat j - 12\hat k$. The value of  \[\alpha \]   for which angular momentum is conserved about origin is
Solution:
For the conservation of angular momentum about origin, the torque about origin will be zero.
By definition, $$\vec \tau  = \vec r \times \vec F$$ 
Here, $\vec r = 2\hat i - 6\hat j - 12\hat k$ 
And $\vec F = \alpha \hat i + 3\hat j + 6\hat k$ 
$\begin{gathered}
  \therefore \,\,\,\vec \tau = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 
  2&{ - 6}&{ - 12} \\ 
  \alpha &3&6 
\end{array}} \right| \\
  \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = \hat i\left( { - 36 + 36} \right) - \hat j\left( {12 + 12\alpha } \right) + \hat k\left( {6 + 6\alpha } \right) \\
  \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = - \hat j\left( {12 + 12\alpha } \right) + \hat k\left( {6 + 6\alpha } \right) \\ 
\end{gathered} $ 
But $\vec \tau = 0$ 
\[\begin{gathered}
  \therefore \,\,12 + 12\alpha = 0\,\,\,\,{\text{or}}\,\,\,\,\alpha = - 1 \\
  {\text{and }}\,\,\,\,6 + 6\alpha = 0\,\,\,\,{\text{or}}\,\,\,\,\alpha = - 1 \\ 
\end{gathered} \]

Question:
Assertion: In rDNA technology, the restriction enzymes that produce sticky ends are commonly used.
Reason: Sticky ends facilitate the action of enzyme DNA ligase.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Both Assertion and Reason are false.
Solution: Restriction enzymes cut the strand of DNA a little away from the centre of the palindrome sites, but between the same two bases on the opposite strands. This leaves single stranded portions at the ends. There are overhanging stretches called sticky ends on each strand. These are named so because they form hydrogen bonds with their complementary cut counterparts. This stickiness of the ends facilitates the action of the enzyme DNA ligase.

Question: The room temperature is 25 °C. In it a body cools from 60 °C to 50 °C in 10 minutes. Assuming Newton’s law of cooling to hold good, the temperature of the body at the end of next 10 minutes will be

Assertion (A): It is easier to spray water in the form of fine droplets if the water is heated.
Reason (R): Surface tension of a liquid generally decreases with an increase in temperature.
Options: Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).
Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is NOT the correct explanation of (A).
Solution: Spraying involves creating a large surface area, which requires work against surface tension $\left( {W = T\Delta A} \right)$. When water is heated, its surface tension ( $T$ ) decreases due to increased molecular thermal activity. Lower surface tension means less work is required to break the bulk liquid into fine droplets.

Question: Which kind of pyramid is represented by the given figure?
Options: Pyramid of numbers in terrestrial ecosystem
Pyramid of biomass in terrestrial ecosystem
Pyramid of biomass in aquatic ecosystem
Pyramid of numbers in aquatic ecosystem
Solution: In a marine aquatic ecosystem, the biomass of phytoplanktons (producers) is smaller than that of zooplanktons (primary consumers) and the biomass of zooplanktons is smaller than that of secondary consumers. This results in the inverted pyramid of biomass in an aquatic ecosystem. This shape is the consequence of the very short life spans of phytoplanktons, which are consumed almost as rapidly as they are formed. Also phytoplanktons have high reproductive potential i.e. they reproduce rapidly.

Question: A disc of mass 2 kg and radius 0.2 m is rotating with angular velocity 30 rad s<sup>-1</sup>. What is the new angular velocity, if a mass 0.25 kg is put on the periphery of the disc?
Solution:
Considering disc and mass as the system.As no external torque acts on the system so angular momentum of the system will be conserved.

$${L_i} = \frac{{M{R^2}}}{2}.{\omega _i}$$

$${L_f} = \left( {\frac{{M{R^2}}}{2} + m{R^2}} \right){\omega _f}$$

As $${L_i} = {L_f}$$

$$\frac{{M{R^2}{\omega _i}}}{2} = \frac{{\left( {M + 2m} \right)}}{2}{R^2}{\omega _f}$$

$${\omega _f} = \frac{{M{\omega _i}}}{{M + 2m}}$$

$${\omega _f} = \frac{{2 \times 30}}{{2.5}}$$

$${\omega _f} = 24\,rad/s$$

2. Metallic oxides are always acidic in nature
3. Amphoteric oxides react with both acids and bases
4. Basic character of oxides increases down the group
Solution:
(1) Correct – PbO₂ is amphoteric
(2) Incorrect – Metallic oxides are generally basic, not acidic
(3) Correct – Amphoteric oxides react with both acids & bases
(4) Correct – Down the group → basic character increases

Question:

Question: Identify the incorrect statement related to Pollination:
Options: Pollination by water is quite rare in flowering plants
Pollination by wind is more common amongst abiotic pollination
Flowers produce foul odours to attract flies and beetles to get pollinated
Moths and butterflies are the most dominant pollinating agents among insects
Solution:
Among the animals, insects particularly bees are the dominant pollinating agents.
Pollination carried out through water is called hydrophily. Pollination by water is quite rare in flowering plants (only 2%).
Pollination by wind is more common amongst abiotic pollination. Wind pollination requires the light and non-sticky pollen grains so that they can be transported in wind currents.
Some plants whose flowers are pollinated by flies and beetles smell this way(foul odour), mainly to attract flies and beetles, which normally lay their eggs in faeces and rotting materials.

Question:
Assertion : Pneumotaxic centre, located in the medulla region of the brain, moderates the respiratory rhythm centre.
Reason : Pneumotaxic centre controls the switch 'ON' point of inspiration.
Options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
Solution: Pneumotaxic centre in the pons region of the brain can moderate functions of the respiratory rhythm centre. Neural signals from this centre can reduce the duration of inspiration and thereby alter the respiratory rate. A chemosensitive area is situated adjacent to the rhythm centre to make necessary adjustments in the respiratory process. Receptors associated with aortic arch and carotid artery also can recognize changes in CO<sub>2</sub> and H<sup>+</sup> concentration and send necessary signals to the rhythm centre for remedial actions. The role of oxygen in the regulation of respiratory rhythm is quite insignificant.

Question: The radii of curvature of the two surfaces of a lens are 30 cm and 20 cm and the refractive index of the material of the lens is 1.5. If the lens is concave-convex, then the focal length of the lens is
Solution:
The focal length of the lens,
$\frac{1}{f} = \left( {\mu - 1} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}} \right)$
$\frac{1}{f} = \left( {1.5 - 1} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{{ - 30}} - \frac{1}{{ - 20}}} \right)$
$ = 0.5\left( {\frac{1}{{20}} - \frac{1}{{30}}} \right)$
$ = 0.5\left( {\frac{{30 - 20}}{{600}}} \right)$
or $\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{{  10}}{{600}} = \frac{{ 1}}{{120}}$
or The focal length, $f = +120\,cm$.

Question: The correct route through which wave of contraction resulting in a impulse travels in the heart is
Options: SA node$ \to $Purkinje fibres$ \to $bundle of His<sub> </sub>$ \to $AV node<sub> </sub>$ \to $<sub> </sub>heart muscles
SA node $ \to $AV node $ \to $bundle of His $ \to $ Purkinje fibres$ \to $heart muscles
AV node$ \to $bundle of His $ \to $SA node$ \to $Purkinje fibres$ \to $heart muscles
AV node$ \to $SA node$ \to $Purkinje fibres$ \to $bundle of His$ \to $heart muscles
Solution:
The electrical impulse (wave of contraction) travels in the following order:

SA node → Pacemaker of heart (initiates impulse)

AV node → Receives impulse from SA node

Bundle of His → Conducts impulse to ventricles

Purkinje fibres → Distribute impulse throughout ventricular walls

Heart muscles → Ventricles contract

Question:
Which statements about sarcomere are correct?
1. Sarcomere extends between two Z-lines.
2. A-band length remains constant during contraction.
3. I-band length increases during contraction.
4. H-zone may disappear during contraction.
Solution:
Statement 1: Correct.
Sarcomere is the functional unit of contraction and extends from one Z-line to the next.
Statement 2: Correct.
A-band corresponds to the length of thick filaments and remains constant during contraction.
Statement 3: Incorrect.
I-band shortens during contraction due to increased overlap of actin and myosin filaments.
Statement 4: Correct.
H-zone shortens and may disappear as overlap increases.
Thus, statements 1, 2 and 4 are correct.

Question:
A slide under microscope shows following features:
(i) Unicellularity
(ii) Well defined nucleus
(iii) Biflagellate-one flagellum lying longitudinally and the other transversely
What would you identify it as?
Solution: Dinoflagellates are basically unicellular, motile and biflagellate, golden brown, photosynthetic protists. The two flagella are different (heterokont), one transverse flagellum and other longitudinal flagellum. The longitudinal flagellum is narrow, smooth, directed posteriorly and lies in the sulcus. The transverse flagellum is ribbon-like and lies in the cingulum or annulus. The two types of flagella beat in different directions. A well defined nucleus is present, which has been named as mesokaryon.

Question: A paramagnetic sample shows a net magnetisation of 0.8 Am-1, when placed in an external magnetic field of strength 0.8 T at a temperature 5K. When the same sample is placed in an external magnetic field of 0.4 T at a temperature of 20 K. The magnetisation is
Solution:
For paramagnetic sample (Curie’s law) I ∝ B/T
where, I1 = 0.8 A/m and B1 = 0.8 T
and T1 = 5K ⇒ B2 = 0.4 T
T2 = 20 K ⇒ I2 =?
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{{{I_1}}}{{{I_2}}} = \frac{{{B_2}/{T_1}}}{{{B_2}/{T_2}}}\,\,\,\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{{{I_1}}}{{{I_2}}} = \frac{{{B_1} \times {T_2}}}{{{B_2} \times {T_1}}}$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{{0.8}}{{{I_2}}} = \frac{{0.8 \times 20}}{{0.4 \times 5}} \Rightarrow \,\,{I_2} = \frac{{0.4 \times 5}}{2} = 0.1\,\,{\text{A}}{{\text{m}}^{ - 1}}$

Question:
Transmission of HIV can not occur by:
1. Transfusion of contaminated blood.
2. Sexual contact with an infected person
3. From an infected mother to her baby through the placenta.
4. Sharing infected needles
5. Sharing food with an infected person
Solution:
• AIDS is caused by the Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV), a member of a group of viruses called retrovirus, which have an envelope enclosing the RNA genome.
• HIV does not spread through sharing food with an infected person.
• Transmission of HIV-infection generally occurs by
(a) Sexual contact with infected person,
(b) By transfusion of contaminated blood and blood products,
(c) By sharing infected needles as in the case of intravenous drug abusers and
(d) From infected mother to her child through placenta.

Question: The age structure of a population influences population growth because
Options: younger females have more offsprings than do older females
different age groups have different reproductive capabilities
more is the number of immature individuals, slower is the growth of population
a shorter generation time results in slower population growth
Solution: Different age groups have different reproductive capabilities. Pre-reproductive individuals are the young individuals which will enter the reproductive age after some time. They are the potential source of increase in population. Reproductive individuals are the ones which are actually adding new members to the population. Post-reproductive individuals are older individuals which no longer take part in reproduction.

Question: An electron moving around the nucleus with an angular momentum \[l\] has a magnetic moment
Solution:
An electron moving around the nucleus has a magnetic moment \[\mu \] given by
\[\mu = \frac{e}{{2m}}l\]
Where, \[l\] is the magnitude of the angular momentum of the circulating electron around the nucleus. The smallest value a \[\mu \] is called the bohr magneton \[{\mu _B}\] and its value is
\[{\mu _B} = 9.27 \times {10^{ - 24}}\]

Question: The sequence of conduction in human heart may be
Options: SA-node, bundle of His, Purkinje fibres AV-node, heart muscle
SA-node, AV-node, Purkinje fibres, bundle of His, heart muscle
SA-node, AV-node, bundle of His, Purkinje fibres, heart muscle
Solution:
SA node → AV node → Bundle of His → Purkinje fibres → Heart muscle

Explanation:

  1. SA node – Natural pacemaker, generates the impulse.

  2. AV node – Receives impulse from SA node.

  3. Bundle of His – Conducts impulse to ventricles.

  4. Purkinje fibres – Distribute impulse throughout ventricular walls.

  5. Heart muscle – Contracts.

Question: Which of the following reactions is an example of a redox reaction? (2017)

Assertion: Equilibrium constant does not depend upon concentration of various reactants, presence of catalyst, direction from which equilibrium is reached.
Reason: Equilibrium constant is only dependent upon the temperature.
Options: Both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
Both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
Assertion is true but reason is false.
Both assertion and reason are false.
Solution:
The equilibrium constant (K) is a characteristic property of a reaction at a given temperature. It is independent of initial concentrations, catalysts, and the path or direction by which equilibrium is achieved.

A catalyst only speeds up the attainment of equilibrium but does not change the value of K.

Since the value of the equilibrium constant depends solely on temperature, the reason correctly explains the assertion.

Question: Which one of the following options gives one correct example of convergent evolution and divergent evolution?
Options: Convergent evolution: Eyes of octopus and mammals; Divergent evolution: Bones of the forelimbs of vertebrates
Convergent evolution: Thorns of Bougainvillea and tendrils of Cucurbita; Divergent evolution: Wings of butterflies and birds
Convergent evolution: Bones of the forelimbs of vertebrates; Divergent evolution: Wings of a butterfly and birds
Convergent evolution: Thorns of Bougainvillea and tendrils of Cucurbita; Divergent evolution: Eyes of octopus and mammals
Solution:
When two species having no common ancestors develop common characters then it is convergent evolution and when two species having common ancestors develop different characters it is divergent evolution.

In the case of wings of birds and butterflies, the physiological appearance or function is the same – meant for flying but the anatomical structure, when observed, is completely different. Such structures are known as analogous structures and they indicate a lack of common ancestry. This is Convergent evolution. Other examples are – Flippers of Dolphins and Penguins and Eyes of Mammals and Octopus.

The forelimbs of mammals, Cheetah, bats, and whales have the same bone arrangements though all of them have different functions. So it means that all these forelimbs in different animals evolved in different directions according to their needs. This is Divergent evolution. Other examples of such evolution are – Heart and brain of vertebrates, Tendrils, and thorns in Cucurbita and Bougainvillea respectively.

Question: The current I in the circuit (see figure) is
Which of the following options is correct about structures visible in the cross section of a centriole?
Options: Peripheral microtubules (triplets)-9; Central microtubules (singlets)-2; Hub-1; Spokes-9; Inter triplet bridge-9
Peripheral microtubules (triplets)-9; Central microtubules (singlets)-2; Hub-9; Spokes-9; Inter triplet bridge-9
Peripheral microtubules (triplets)-9; Central microtubules (singlets)-2; Hub-1; Spokes-2; Inter triplet bridge-2
Peripheral microtubules (triplets)-9; Central microtubules (singlets)-0; Hub-1; Spokes-9; Inter triplet bridge-9
Solution: A centriole possesses a whorl of nine peripheral fibrils. Fibrils are absent in the centre. The arrangement is, therefore, called 9 + 0 which runs parallel to one another but at an angle of 400. Each fibril is made up of three sub-fibres called triplet fibril. The three sub-fibres are in reality microtubules joined together by their margins and therefore, share the common walls made of 2-3 protofilaments. The centre of a centriole possesses a rod-shaped proteinaceous mass known as hub. The hub has a diameter of 2.5 nm. From the hub, develops 9 proteinaceous strands towards the peripheral triplet fibrils called radial fibres or spokes.

Question: An older-model car accelerates from 0 to speed v in a time interval of \[\Delta t.\]A newer, More powerful sports car accelerates from 0 to 2v in the same time period. Assuming the energy coming from the engine appears only as kinetic energy of the cars and both the car have a same masses. Choose correct statement.
Options: The power of the sports car is four times that of the older-model car.
The power of the sports car is two times that of the older-model car.
The power of the sports car is equal to that of the older-model car.
The power of the sports car cannot be compared from given data.
Solution:
\[P = \frac{W}{{\Delta t}}\]
Older-model: \[W = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} \Rightarrow {P_{older}} = \frac{{\frac{{m{v^2}}}{2}}}{{\Delta t}} = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{{2\Delta t}}\]
Newer-model: \[W = \frac{1}{2}m{\left( {2v} \right)^2} = \frac{1}{2}\left( {4m{v^2}} \right)\]
\[ \to {P_{newer}} = \frac{{4m{v^2}}}{{2\Delta t}} = 4\frac{{m{v^2}}}{{2\Delta t}}\]
Hence the power of the sports car is four times that of the older-model car.

Question: The graph between the resistive force F acting on a body and the distance covered by the body is shown in the figure. The mass of the body is 25kg and initial velocity is 2 m/s. When the distance covered by the body is 4m, its kinetic energy would be

A body of mass m is moving in a circle of radius r with a constant speed v. The force on the body is $\frac{{m{v^2}}}{r}$ and is directed towards the centre. What is the work done by this force in moving the body over half the circumference of the circle?
Solution:
Work done by centripetal force is always zero, because force and instantaneous displacement are always mutually perpendicular.
$W = \overrightarrow F .\overrightarrow s = Fs\cos \theta = Fs\cos (90^\circ ) = 0$

Question: Which one of the following is categorised as a parasite in true sense?
Options: The female Anopheles bites and sucks blood from humans
Human foetus developing inside the uterus draws nourishment from the mother
Head louse living on the human scalp as well as laying eggs on human hair
The cuckoo (koel) lays its eggs in crow's nest
Solution: A parasite in the true sense is said to species that lives, eats, reproduces, and brings harm upon its hosts. The female Anopheles bites and sucks blood from humans as food and does not live on them. A human fetus developing inside the uterus drawing nourishment from the mother is a form of reproduction and it does not provide any harm to the mother. The cuckoo laying its eggs in the crow's nest is a form of brood parasitism. Head louse living on the human scalp is a form of true parasite because it lives, takes nourishment, and reproduces on the human scalp.

Statement I is true, Statement II is false.

Question:

Choose the correct option
Solution: Earlier, insulin was extracted from the pancreas of slaughtered cattle and pigs but some patients began developing allergies. Bacteria can not be made to synthesize insulin from the human gene because of the presence of introns. The human gene contains both noncoding introns and a coding sequence called exons. Question: Two boxes A and B of masses m and M are suspended by a system of pulleys are in equilibrium as shown. Express M in terms of m.
Solution:
Since tension on both sides of a pulley are equal and string is massless therefore tension everywhere on the string must have same magnitude

For equilibrium of block A \[\Sigma \vec F = \vec 0 \Rightarrow T = mg\] ……(i)
For equilibrium of pulley attached to block B \[\Sigma \vec F = \vec 0 \Rightarrow F = 2T\] …..(ii)
For equilibrium of block B \[\Sigma \vec F = \vec 0 \Rightarrow F = Mg\] …..(iii)
From equation (i),(ii) and (iii), we have M = 2m

Question: A cannon on a level plane is aimed at an angle $\theta $ above the horizontal and a shell is fired with a muzzle velocity ${v_0}$ towards a vertical cliff a distance D away. Then the height from the bottom at which the shell strikes the side walls of the cliff is
Solution:
Equation of trajectory for oblique projectile motion
$y = x\tan \theta - \frac{{g{x^2}}}{{2{u^2}{{\cos }^2}\theta }}$
Substituting $x = D$ and $u = {v_0}$
$h = D\tan \theta - \frac{{g{D^2}}}{{2u_0^2{{\cos }^2}\theta }}.$

Question:
Which of the following are correct?
(i) Sponges: Cellular level of organization
(ii) Cnidaria: Tissue level of organization
(iii) Platyhelminthes: Organ level of organization
(iv) Annelids, Arthropods, Molluscs, Echinoderms and Chordates: Organ system level of organization.
Solution: Animals show four different grades of organization. (i) Protoplasmic grade found in protozoans. (ii) Cellular grade is the characteristic of sponges and mesozoans because in a sponge, cells exhibit division of labour for performing specialized functions. (iii) Tissue grade is of two types: Cell-tissue grade ls seen in coelenterates as there are cells not only specialized for different functions but also certain similar cells gather together to form tissue as well. Tissue - organ grade appears in flatworms (Platyhelminthes) with arrangement of tissues to form organs. (iv) In organ system level of organisation, organs are joined in a system to perform basic functions. It is found in higher invertebrates and all vertebrates.

Question: The correct order of equivalent conductance at infinite dilution of LiCl, NaCl and KCl is
Solution:
The cations in dilute solution undergo hydration as they are  surrounded by water molecules. So, smaller the cation, more will be the hydration of cation and hence less is the mobility which leads to decrease in  equivalent conductance.  
In group 1, down the group, size of alkali metal cation increases and the extent of hydration decreases, due to which equivalent conductance increases.
So, the correct order of equivalent conductance at infinite dilution of LiCl, NaCl and KCl is:

                                                 KCl  >  NaCl  >  LiCl

Question: Which of the following statements are incorrect regarding ecological pyramids?
Options: In most ecosystems, carnivores are higher in number and biomass than herbivores
Energy content gradually increases from first to fourth trophic level.
In the sea ecosystem, the biomass of fishes far exceeds that of phytoplankton.
Pyramids of energy are always upright.
Solution: In most ecosystems, herbivores are higher in number and biomass than carnivores. Energy content gradually decreases from the first to fourth trophic level as some energy is always lost as heat in each step. So, the pyramid of energy is always upright. In a sea ecosystem, the biomass of fishes far exceeds that of phytoplankton, hence, in the sea, the pyramid of biomass is always inverted.

Question: Diagram shows 3 vectors which sum to zero, all of equal length. Which statement below is true
Solution:
$\vec A + \vec B + \vec C = 0$

Add $\vec A$ to both sides
$2\vec A + \vec B + \vec C = \vec A$
Subtract $\vec B$ from both sides
$2\vec A + \vec C = \vec A - \vec B$
or
$\vec A - \vec B = 2\vec A + \vec C$
Alternate:
$\begin{gathered}
  \vec A - \vec B = 2\vec A + \vec C \\
  0 = 2\vec A - \vec A + \vec C + \vec B \\
  0 = \vec A + \vec B + \vec C \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: Which of the following tools of recombinant DNA technology is incorrectly paired with its use?
Options: DNA ligase - Multiplication of rDNA molecules
Taq polymerase - In polymerase chain reaction to amplify sections of DNA
Solution: DNA ligases are also called genetic gum. They join two individual fragments of double stranded DNA by forming phosphodiester bonds between them, thus help in sealing of DNA fragments. Therefore, this acts as a molecular glue. The enzyme used most often is T<sub>4</sub> DNA ligase.

Question:
Which of following contains the key tools for recombinant DNA technology?
(i) Restriction endonucleases, ligases, vectors
(ii) Ligases, host organism, polymerase enzymes
(iii) Vectors, Taq polymerase, primers
(iv) Restriction exonucleases, ligases, primers, bioreactors
Solution: Recombinant DNA technology has five steps: (1) cutting the desired DNA by restriction sites using restriction endonucleases, (2) amplifying the gene copies by PCR, (3) inserting the genes into the vectors and sealing gaps in DNA using ligase, (4) transferring the vectors into the host organism, and (5) obtaining the products of recombinant genes by the action of polymerase enzymes.

Question:
For the gas phase reaction
2 NO \[ \rightleftharpoons \] N2 + O2, ∆H = – 43.5 Kcal mol–1which one of the statement below is true for

N2 (g) + O2 (g) \[ \rightleftharpoons \] 2 NO (g)
Options: K varies with the addition of NO.
Solution:
As the enthalpy of the given reaction is negative, so, the enthalpy of its reverse reaction is positive, hence, it is an endothermic reaction.
So, according to the Le-Chatelier’s principle, when the temperature decreases, the reaction is directed in backwards direction for an endothermic reaction. Due to which K of the reaction decreases.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.

Question:
Match the Dipole properties (Dipole Moment $\vec p$ ) in Column I with Column II:
Column IColumn II
ATorque $\left( {\vec \tau } \right)$P $ - \vec p \cdot \vec E$
BPotential Energy $\left( U \right)$Q $\vec p \times \vec E$
C$E$ at axial point (short dipole)R $\frac{{kp}}{{{r^3}}}$
D$E$ at equatorial point (short)S $\frac{{2kp}}{{{r^3}}}$
Solution: Torque on a dipole is $\vec \tau = \vec p \times \vec E$. Potential energy is $U = - pE{\text{cos}}\theta $. The electric field on the axial line is twice the magnitude of the field on the equatorial line for the same distance $r\left( {r \gg a} \right)$.

Question:
Consider the following statements regarding the reaction of phenol with CHCl₃ in the presence of KOH (Reimer–Tiemann reaction):
Statement I: Dichlorocarbene (:CCl₂) is formed as an intermediate in the reaction.
Statement II: Carbon tetrachloride (CCl₄) acts as the electrophile in this reaction.
Options: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Solution: In the Reimer–Tiemann reaction, dichlorocarbene (:CCl₂) is generated from chloroform in the presence of strong base. This carbene acts as the electrophile that attacks the activated phenol ring. CCl₄ is not involved as an electrophile, so Statement II is incorrect.

Question:
Consider a wire of length $L$ and area $A$ stretched by force $F$ :
Statement I: The elongation $\Delta L$ produced in a wire is directly proportional to its original length.
Statement II: If the radius of the wire is doubled, the elongation for the same force becomes half.
Select the correct option:
Options: Statement I is true, Statement II is false.
Statement I is false, Statement II is true.
Solution: $\Delta L = \frac{{FL}}{{AY}}$. Thus $\Delta L \propto L$ (I is true). However, $\Delta L \propto \frac{1}{{{r^2}}}$. If radius is doubled, $\Delta L$becomes 1/4th, not half (II is false).

$ - N{H_2}, - OH, - {O^ \ominus }, - NH - CO - C{H_3}$ all these compound are +M and -I groups
Solution:
In (a), compound B is more stable carbocation due to the +M effect of the -OCH3 group.
In (b), compound A has more alpha hydrogen atoms, hence more hyperconjugation the more will be the stability of alkenes and less will be the heat of hydrogenation.
In (c), Only -O- is a strong +M and +I group.

Question:
Which of the following statement(s) regarding energy flow is/are false?
I. Detritus food chain begins with dead organic matter
II. In an aquatic ecosystem, the detritus food chain is the major conduit for energy flow
III. In a terrestrial ecosystem a larger fraction of energy flows through the grazing food chain
IV. Producers belong to the first trophic level of the food chain
Solution: In an aquatic ecosystem, the grazing food chain(GFC) is the major conduit for energy flow. The starting point in a grazing food chain are the producers- plants and the first energy transfer is from plants to herbivores. In the terrestrial ecosystem, a much larger fraction of energy flows through the detritus food chain than through the grazing food chain.

Question: Two simple pendulums of length 5 m and 20 m respectively are given small linear displacement in one direction at the same time. They will again be in the phase when the pendulum of shorter length has completed n oscillations. Value of n is:
Solution:
If t is the time taken by pendulums to come in same phase again first time after $t = 0$and ${N_S} = $ Number of oscillations made by shorter length pendulum with time period ${T_S}$.
${N_L} = $ Number of oscillations made by longer length pendulum with time period ${T_L}$.
Then $t = {N_S}{T_S} = {N_L}{T_L}$
$ \Rightarrow $ ${N_S}2\pi \sqrt {\frac{5}{g}} = {N_L} \times 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{20}}{g}} $ ($\because T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{g}} $)
$ \Rightarrow $ ${N_S} = 2{N_L}$ i.e. if ${N_L} = 1$ $ \Rightarrow $ ${N_S} = 2$

Question: In an experiment of single slit diffraction pattern, first minimum for red light coincides with first maximum of some other wavelength. If wavelength of red light is \[6600\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^ \circ  \] then wavelength for first maximum (another light) will be:
Solution:
In a single slit experiment,
For diffraction maxima,
\[a\sin \theta = \left( {2n + 1} \right)\frac{\lambda }{2}\]
And for diffraction minima,
\[a\sin \theta = n\lambda \]
According to question,
\[\begin{gathered}
  \left( {2 \times 1 + 1} \right)\frac{\lambda }{2} = 1 \times 6600 \\
  \left( {\because \,\,{\lambda _R} = 6600\,\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^ \circ } \right) \\
  \lambda = \frac{{6600 \times 2}}{3} \\
  \lambda = 4400\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^ \circ \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: Identify the wrong statement with regard to restriction enzymes.
Options: They are useful in genetic engineering
Sticky ends can be joined by using endonucleases
Each restriction enzyme functions by inspecting the length of a DNA sequence
They cut the strand of DNA at palindromic sites
Solution:
(a) Genetic engineering or recombinant DNA technology can be accomplished only if we have the key tools, i.e., restriction enzymes, polymerase enzymes, ligases, vectors and the host organism.
(b) The sticky ends in DNA that result from the action of restriction endonucleases cannot be joined by the restriction enzymes, but by DNA ligases. 
(c) Restriction enzymes begin their action by first scanning the length of the DNA sequence and then recognising a specific sequence. This specific base sequence is known as the recognition sequence.
(d) Each restriction endonuclease recognises a specific palindromic nucleotide sequence in the DNA.

Question: In a patient the stroke volume was found to be 60 mL and the cardiac output is 6 litres. What can be deduced about the rate of heartbeat of this person?
Options: Heart is beating slower than normal
Heart is beating faster than normal
Rate of heart beat is in the normal range
Solution:
Stroke volume x Rate of heartbeat = Cardiac output
Therefore, Rate of heartbeat = Cardiac output / Stroke volume
= 6 litres/60 mL 
= 6000 mL / 60 mL
= 100 beats per minute.

But the normal heart rate is 72 beats per minute.
Therefore, in this patient, the heart is beating faster than normal.

3. Chiasmata become visible during diplotene.
4. Terminalisation occurs during pachytene.
Solution:
1. Correct - Synaptonemal complex forms in zygotene.

3. Correct - Chiasmata visible in diplotene.
4. Incorrect - Terminalisation occurs in diplotene/diakinesis, not pachytene.

Question:
The degree of hydrolysis in hydrolytic equilibrium
\[{A^ - } + {H_2}O \rightleftharpoons HA + O{H^ - }\]
At salt concentration of \[0.001M\] is: \[\left( {{K_a} = 1 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}} \right)\]

Match Column I with Column II.
Column – IColumn -II
(A) \[{H_2}\left( g \right) + {I_2}\left( g \right) \rightleftharpoons 2Hl\left( g \right)\] (1)\[{K_p} = {K_c}\left[ {RT} \right]\]
(B)\[{N_2}\left( g \right) + 3{H_2}\left( g \right) \rightleftharpoons 2N{H_3}\left( g \right)\](2)\[{K_p} = {K_c}{\left[ {RT} \right]^2}\]
(C)\[PC{l_5}\left( g \right) \rightleftharpoons PC{l_3}\left( g \right) + C{l_2}\left( g \right)\](3)\[{K_p} = {K_c}{\left[ {RT} \right]^{ - 2}}\]
(D)\[N{H_4}Cl\left( s \right) \rightleftharpoons N{H_3}\left( g \right) + HCl\left( g \right)\](4) \[{K_p} = {K_c}\]
Solution:
(A) \[\begin{gathered}
  {H_{2\left( g \right)}} + {I_{2\left( g \right)}} \rightleftharpoons 2H{I_{\left( g \right)}} \\
  \Delta n = \left( {gas\,\,\left( p \right) - gas\left( R \right) = \left( {2 - 2} \right)} \right) = 0 \\
  {K_p} = {K_C} \\
\end{gathered} \]
(B) \[\begin{gathered}
  {N_{2\left( g \right)}} + 3{H_{2\left( g \right)}} \rightleftharpoons 2N{H_{\left( g \right)}} \\
  \Delta n = \left( {2 - 4} \right) = - 2 \\

(D) \[\begin{gathered}
  N{H_4}C{l_{\left( c \right)}} \rightleftharpoons {H_3}_{\left( g \right)} + HC{l_{\left( g \right)}} \\

Question:
Statement I: In the Etard reaction, toluene on oxidation with chromyl chloride (CrO₂Cl₂) in a non-aqueous solvent gives benzaldehyde after hydrolysis.
Statement II: In this reaction, the methyl group of toluene is completely oxidized to benzoic acid.
Options: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Solution:
In the Etard reaction, an aromatic methyl group is selectively oxidized using chromyl chloride (CrO₂Cl₂) in solvents such as CCl₄ or CS₂. Toluene forms a complex with chromyl chloride which on hydrolysis produces benzaldehyde.
The oxidation stops at the aldehyde stage and does not proceed to benzoic acid under these conditions. Hence Statement I is correct and Statement II is incorrect.

Question: A wave is expressed by the equation $y = 0.5\sin \pi \left( {0.01x - 3t} \right),$ where y and x are in metre and t in second. Its speed of propagation is
Solution:
$y = 0.5\sin \left( {\frac{\pi }{{100}}x - 3\pi t} \right)\left( {{\text{given}}} \right)$
Comparing with the equation $y = A\sin \left( {kx - \omega t} \right),$
We have $k = \frac{\pi }{{100}}$ and $\omega = 3\pi \,{\text{rad}}{s^{ - 1}}$
$\therefore v = \frac{\omega }{k} = \frac{{3\pi }}{{\pi /100}} = 300\,m{s^{ - 1}}$

Question: Type of isomerism exhibited by [Cr(NCS)(NH3)5] [ZnCl4] :
At room temperature, HCl is a gas while HF is a low boiling liquid. This is because
Solution: Due to the high electronegativity and the small size of fluorine, it is capable of forming hydrogen bond between the H – F molecules; whereas, in the case of H – Cl, the intermolecular force is the relatively weaker dipole –dipole force of attraction. Thus, at room temperature, HCl is a gas and HF is a liquid.

Question: The sum of denticity of ligands present in K<sub>2</sub>[Co(en)<sub>2</sub>C<sub>2</sub>O<sub>4</sub>] is
Solution:
The question asks for the sum of the denticity of all ligands present in the complex K₂[Co(en)₂C₂O₄].

First, let's identify the ligands in the coordination sphere, which is [Co(en)₂C₂O₄]²⁻.

  1. Ligand 1: 'en'
    'en' is the abbreviation for ethylenediamine (H₂N-CH₂-CH₂-NH₂). It has two donor nitrogen atoms, which means it can form two coordinate bonds with the central metal atom. Therefore, ethylenediamine is a bidentate ligand. Its denticity is 2.
    The formula has two such ligands, (en)₂.
  2. Ligand 2: 'C₂O₄'
    'C₂O₄' is the oxalate ion (⁻OOC-COO⁻). It has two donor oxygen atoms and can also form two coordinate bonds with the central metal atom. Therefore, the oxalate ion is also a bidentate ligand. Its denticity is 2.
    The formula has one such ligand.

Now, let's calculate the sum of the denticities of all the individual ligand molecules attached to the central cobalt atom:

Sum of denticities = (Number of 'en' ligands × Denticity of 'en') + (Number of 'C₂O₄' ligands × Denticity of 'C₂O₄')
Sum of denticities = (2 × 2) + (1 × 2)
Sum of denticities = 4 + 2 = 6

The sum of the denticities of the ligands is also equal to the coordination number of the central metal ion. In this case, the coordination number of Co is 6.

The correct option is (c) 6.

Question: A car A is travelling on a straight level road with a uniform speed of 60$km/h.$It is followed by another car B which is moving with a speed of 70 $km/h.$When the distance between them is 2.5 km, the car B is given a deceleration of 20$km/{h^2}.$After how much time will B catch up with A ?
Solution:
Let car B catches, car A after ‘t’ hour, then
$60t + 2.5 = 70t - \frac{1}{2} \times 20 \times {t^2}$
⇒ $10{t^2} - 10t + 2.5 = 0$⇒ ${t^2} - t + 0.25 = 0$
$\therefore $ $t = \frac{{1 \pm \sqrt {1 - 4 \times (0.25)} }}{2} = \frac{1}{2}hr$

Question: Which of the following statements is incorrect?
Options: In a majority of flowering plants, one of the megaspores is functional while the other three degenerate.
Only the functional megaspore develops into the female gametophyte (embryo sac).
The nucleus of the functional megaspore divides mitotically to form two nuclei which move to the opposite poles, forming the 4-nucleate embryo sac.
Three cells are grouped together at the micropylar end and constitute the egg apparatus.
Solution: The nucleus of the functional megaspore divides mitotically to form two nuclei which move to the opposite poles, forming the 2-nucleate embryo sac. Two more sequential mitotic nuclear divisions result in the formation of the 4-nucleate and later the 8-nucleate stages of the embryo sac.

Question: The term used for transfer of pollen grains from anthers of one plant to stigma of a different plant which, during pollination, brings genetically different types of pollen grains to stigma, is
Solution:
Transfer of pollen grains (shed from the anther) to the stigma of a pistil is termed pollination. Xenogamy (cross-pollination) is the transfer of pollen grains from anther to the stigma of a different plant. This is the only type of pollination that brings genetically different types of pollen grains to the stigma.
-In some plants such as, Viola (common pansy), Oxalis, and Commelina, two types of flowers are produced– chasmogamous flowers that are similar to flowers of other species with exposed anthers and stigma, and cleistogamous flowers that do not open at all, ensuring self-pollination.
-Geitonogamy is the transfer of pollen grains from the anther to the stigma of another flower of the same plant.

Question: Select the option that correctly identifies A and B in the given figure.
Solution: The given diagram represents Funaria, a moss. Mosses are placed under Bryophytes. In Funaria, the predominant stage of the life cycle is the gametophyte.  Gametophyte produces gametes.  The male gametes (antherozoids) are fused with the egg (female gamete) to produce the zygote which produces a multicellular body called a sporophyte. The sporophyte is attached to the photosynthetic gametophyte and derives nourishment from it.

Question: A particle executes simple harmonic motion with a frequency f. The frequency with which its kinetic energy oscillates is-
Solution:
Let $$x = A\sin \omega t$$

$$v = \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = A\omega \cos \omega t$$

Kinetic energy, $$K = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow K = \frac{1}{2}m{\omega ^2}{A^2}{\cos ^2}\omega t$$

$$ \Rightarrow K = \frac{1}{2}m{\omega ^2}{A^2}\left( {\frac{{1 + \cos 2\omega t}}{2}} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow K = \frac{1}{4}m{\omega ^2}{A^2}\left( {1 + {{\cos }2}\omega t} \right)$$

$$\therefore \omega ' = 2\omega $$

\[\therefore {\rm{Frequency}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{oscillation}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{K}}{\rm{.E}} = 2f\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \left[ {\therefore f = \frac{\omega }{{2\pi }}} \right]\]

Question:
Consider the following statements and choose the correct ones-
1. Diaphragm contraction increases the volume of thoracic chamber in anterio - posterior axis
2. Intrapulmonary pressure falls below atmospheric pressure during inspiration
3. Intrapleural pressure becomes positive during normal inspiration
4. Contraction of external intercostals elevate ribs & sternum causing an increase of volume of thoracic chamber in dorso-ventral axis
Solution:
Statement 1:
When diaphragm contracts, it flattens → increases vertical thoracic dimension → volume ↑ → pressure ↓ (Boyle’s law)
Statement 2:
Air moves from high to low pressure. During inspiration, intrapulmonary pressure becomes slightly less than atmospheric pressure (~1–2 mmHg lower)
Statement 3:
Intrapleural pressure is always negative (around –4 mmHg at rest).
If it becomes positive → lung collapse (pneumothorax)
Statement 4:
External intercostals lift ribs upward and outward → increase AP diameter

Question:
Find out the correct matching:
A. Palmately compound leaves – Leaf let attached to common axis rachis
B. Phyllotaxy – Pattern of arrangement of leaves on stem or branch
C. Pinnately compound leaves – Leaflet attached to a common point i.e. at the tip of petiole
D. Venation – Arrangement of veins and veinlets in the lamina of leaf
Solution: In palmately compound leaves, the leaflets are attached at a common point, i.e., at the tip of the petiole, as in silk cotton. Phyllotaxy is the pattern of arrangement of leaves on the stem or branch. In a pinnately compound leaf, a number of leaflets are present on a common axis, the rachis, which represents the midrib of the leaf as in neem. The arrangement of veins and the veinlets in the lamina of leaf is termed as venation.

Question: The increasing order of the first ionization enthalpies of the elements B, P, S and F (lowest first) is
Solution:
$\begin{gathered}
  {\text{Element}}:{\text{ B}},\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{S}},\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{P}},\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{F}} \\
  {\text{Le}}({\text{eV}}):{\text{ }}8.3,\,\,10.4,\,\,11.0,\,\,17.4 \\
\end{gathered} $
In general, as we move from left to right in a period, the ionization enthalpy increases with increasing atomic number. The ionization enthalpy decreases as we move down a group. P (1s2 2s2 2p6 3s2 3p3) has a stable half-filled electronic configuration than S (1s2 2s2 2p6 3s2 3p4).
For this reason, ionization enthalpy of P is higher than S.

Question: A man covers a distance of 20m with 2m/s and 40m with 5m/s. Find the average speed of the man.
Solution:
Distance covered in point -1 (d1) = 20 m.
Time taken (t1) \[ = \frac{{{d_1}}}{{{\text{velocit}}{{\text{y}}_{\text{1}}}}} = \frac{{20}}{2}\]
\[ = \,10{\text{s}}\]
Distance covered in point -2 (d2) = 40 m.
Time taken (t2) \[ = \frac{{{d_2}}}{{{\text{velocit}}{{\text{y}}_2}}} = \frac{{40}}{5}\]
\[ = \,8{\text{s}}\]
\[{\text{average speed = }}\frac{{{\text{Total distance covered}}}}{{{\text{Total time taken}}}}\]
\[{\text{ = }}\frac{{40 + 20}}{{10 + 8}} = \frac{{60}}{{18}} = \frac{{10}}{3}{\text{m/s}}\]

Question:
Which of the following statements is correct for a cyclic process?
I. The total change in internal energy $\left( {\Delta U} \right)$ over a complete cycle is zero.
II. The net heat absorbed by the system is equal to the net work done by the system.
Solution: Internal energy is a state function; for any cyclic process, $\Delta U = 0$. From the First Law$\left( {\Delta Q = \Delta U + W} \right)$, if $\Delta U = 0$ then $\Delta Q = W$.

Question:
Find out the total number of correct statements:
A. Cyanobacteria have chlorophyll ‘a’ like green plants.
B. Bacteria which oxidizes various inorganic substance such as nitrates and ammonia and
uses the released energy for ATP production are chemosynthetic autotrophic bacteria.
C. Heterotrophic bacteria are less in abundance in nature.
D. Majority of heterotrophic bacteria are decomposers.
E. Chemosynthetic autotrophic bacteria play an important role in recycling of nutrients like nitrogen, phosphorus, iron and sulfur.
Solution:
Cyanobacteria have chlorophyll ‘a’, similar to green plants and are photosynthetic autotrophs. Chemosynthetic autotrophic bacteria oxidize various inorganic substances such as
nitrates, nitrites and ammonia and use the released energy for their ATP production. Heterotrophic bacteria are most abundant in nature. The majority are important decomposers. Chemosynthetic autotrophic bacteria play an important role in recycling of nutrients like nitrogen, phosphorus, iron and sulfur.

Question:
Consider the statements a-d with respect to plasmids. How many of the statements are correct?
a. Plasmids are  usually circular, extra-chromosomal DNA.
b. A plasmid can be used as vector to deliver an alien piece of DNA into the host organism.
c. Plasmids  have the ability to replicate within bacterial cells independent of the control of chromosomal DNA.
d. Plasmids have their own origin of replication.
Solution:
Plasmids are extra chromosomal, self–replicating, usually circular, double -stranded DNA molecules, found in many bacteria and some yeast. They may occur as one or two copies or in multiple copies (500–700) inside the host organism. These plasmid DNA act as vectors to
transfer the piece of DNA attached to it. They have their own origin of replication. They have the ability to replicate within bacterial cells independent of the control of chromosomal DNA.

1. Dominance

5. Polygenic inheritance
Solution:
Inheritance in the human blood group shows multiple alleles, dominance as well as codominance.

2. Within the ABO Blood Group system, the A and B genes are co-dominant, i.e. these will be expressed whenever the gene is present. The O gene is silent and only expressed when neither A nor B is present.

When you are trying to determine whether a plant is a gymnosperm or an angiosperm based on its morphology, which of the following would be an incorrect argument to use?
Options: The plant bears flowers therefore it is an angiosperm
The plant produces seeds therefore it is an angiosperm
The leaves of the tree are needle-like therefore it is a gymnosperm
The plant produces naked seeds therefore it is a gymnosperm
Solution: The distinguishing morphological characteristics between gymnosperms are the flower, leaf and fruit structures. Angiosperms bear flowers, have flat leaves and their seed is covered by a fruit. Gymnosperms do not bear flowers, have needle-like leaves and their seeds are naked. Thus, both plant types produce seeds, but of different types. Therefore, one cannot conclude whether a plant is a gymnosperm or angiosperm by the ability to produce seeds as it is not a distinguishing characteristic.

Question: Identify the incorrect statement with respect to the reproductive system in frogs.
Options: Male reproductive organs consist of a pair of yellowish ovoid testes.
Vasa efferentia are 4-6 in number that arise from testes.
The cloaca is a small, median chamber that is used to pass faecal matter, urine and sperms to the exterior.
Testes are found adhered to the upper part of kidneys by a double fold of peritoneum called mesorchium.
Solution: Male reproductive organs consist of a pair of yellowish ovoid testes, which are found adhered to the upper part of kidneys by a double fold of peritoneum called mesorchium. Vasa efferentia are 10-12 in number that arise from testes. They enter the kidneys on their side and open into Bidder’s canal. Finally it communicates with the urinogenital duct that comes out of the kidneys and opens into the cloaca. The cloaca is a small, median chamber that is used to pass faecal matter, urine and sperms to the exterior.

Question: A series LCR circuit containing a resistance of 120 Ω has resonant angular frequency 4 × 10<sup>5</sup> rad s<sup>-1</sup>. The voltages, at resonance, across the resistance and inductance are 60 V and 40 V respectively. The values of L and C respectively are
Solution:
At resonance $${X_L} = {X_C},\,i = \frac{{{V_R}}}{R} = \frac{{60}}{{120}} = 0.5\,A$$

$${V_L} = i{X_L} = i{\omega _0}L \Rightarrow L = \frac{{{V_L}}}{{i{\omega _0}}} = \frac{{40}}{{0.5 \times 4 \times {{10}^5}}} = 0.2\,mH$$

$${\omega _0} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {LC} }} \Rightarrow C = \frac{1}{{L{\omega _0}^2}} = \frac{1}{{0.2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times {{\left( {4 \times {{10}^5}} \right)}^2}}}$$

$$ = \frac{1}{{32}}\mu F = 0.03125\,\mu F$$

Question:
Match the columns:
Column IColumn IIColumn III
AHypogynousXGynoecium occupies the highest position1Mustard, Brinjal, China rose
BEpigynousYGynoecium is situated in the center2Plum, peach, rose
CPerigynousZMargin of thalamus grows upward enclosing ovary completely3Guava, cucumber, ray florets of sunflower
Solution: In the hypogynous flower the gynoecium occupies the highest position while the other parts are situated below it. The ovary in such flowers is said to be superior, e.g., mustard, china rose and brinjal. If gynoecium is situated in the center and other parts of the flower are located on the rim of the thalamus almost at the same level, it is called perigynous. The ovary here is said to be half inferior, e.g., plum, rose, peach. In epigynous flowers, the margin of thalamus grows upward enclosing the ovary completely and getting fused with it, the other parts of flower arise above the ovary. Hence, the ovary is said to be inferior as in flowers of guava and cucumber, and the ray florets of sunflower.

Question: A monochromatic light is incident at a certain angle on an equilateral triangular prism and suffers minimum deviation. If the refractive index of the material of the prism is \[\sqrt 3 \], then the angle of incidence is
Solution:
Given, refractive index of material of prism \[n = \sqrt 3 \], prism \[A = 60^\circ \]
Method 1
Using prism formula,
\[n = \frac{{\sin \left( {\frac{{A + \delta }}{2}} \right)}}{{\sin \left( {\frac{A}{2}} \right)}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\sqrt 3 = \frac{{\sin \left( {\frac{{60 + \delta }}{2}} \right)}}{{\sin 30^\circ }}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\sin \left( {\frac{{60 + \delta }}{2}} \right) = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\sin \left( {\frac{{60 + \delta }}{2}} \right) = \sin 60^\circ \]
Or \[\frac{{60 + \delta }}{2} = 60\]
Or angle of minimum deviation\[\delta = 60^\circ \]
Incident angle, \[i = \frac{{60 + \delta }}{2} = 60^\circ \]
Method 2 for minimum deviation, ray should pass symmetrically (i.e. parallel to the base of the equilateral prism)
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\]From geometry of given figure, we have,\[r = 30^\circ \]

Using Snell’s law,
\[n = \frac{{\sin i}}{{\sin r}}\]
\[\sin i = n\sin r = \sqrt 3 \sin 30^\circ \]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\sin i = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}\]or \[i = 60^\circ \]

Question: Two charges +10\[\mu \]C and -10\[\mu \]C are held 1mm apart. Calculate the magnitude of electric field at a point on the equatorial line at a distance of 100 cm from the center of the dipole.
Solution:
Given $$q =  \pm 10\,\mu c =  \pm 10 \times {10^{ - 6}}{\text{c}}$$

$$2a = 1\,\,{\text{mm}} = {10^{ - 3}}{\text{m}} \Rightarrow a = 0.5 \times {10^{ - 3}}{\text{m}}$$

$$r = 100\,{\text{cm}} = 1\,{\text{m}}$$

$${\text{As  }}a <  < r{\text{,}}\,\,{\text{therefore  electric field on equational line of a dipole,}}$$

$$E = \frac{{kp}}{{{r^3}}} = \frac{{kq\left( {2a} \right)}}{{{r^3}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow E = \frac{{9 \times {{10}^9} \times 10 \times {{10}^{ - 6}} \times 2 \times 0.5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{{{\left( 1 \right)}^3}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow E = 90\,\,{\text{N/C}}\,\,\left( {{\text{opposite}}\,\,\,{\text{to}}\,\,\,{\text{dipole}}\,\,{\text{moment}}} \right)$$

Question:
Biopatents are
(i) Right to use invention
(ii) Right to use biological entities
(iii) Right to use products
(iv) Right to use process
Solution:
A patent is a right granted by a government to an inventor to prevent others from the commercial use of his invention. When patents are granted for biological entities and for products derived from them, these are called biopatents.
Biopatents grant:
- Right to use the invention
- Right to use biological entities
- Right to use products

Question: If emf induced in a coil is 2 V by changing the current in it from 8A to 6 A in 2×10<sup>–</sup><sup>3</sup> s, then the coefficient of self-induction is
Solution:
Induced emf, e = 2V

$${i_1} = 8A,\,\,{i_2} = 6\,A$$

$$\Delta t = 2 \times {10^{ - 3}}s$$

Coefficient of self-induction,

$$L = \frac{{ - e}}{{\Delta i/\Delta t}} = \frac{{ - 2}}{{\left( {6 - 8} \right)/2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}$$

$$ = \frac{{ - 2 \times 2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{ - 2}}$$

$$ = 2 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,H$$
